coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of octadecylamine-derived carbon dots and application in reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Qi
Hou, Xiudan
Lv, Haitao
Li, Hui
Zhao, Liang
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: In order to make up for the deficiencies of traditional C18 column for separating strong polar compounds, combined with the good hydrophilicity of carbon dots (CDs), novel octadecylamine-derived CDs denoted as C18-CDs are designed, synthesized and applied in RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography with good separation performance towards both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds. C18-CDs are synthesized by simple one-step solvothermal method using octadecylamine and citric acid as carbon sources, and C18-CDs with proper polarity are collected through column chromatography purification. This C18-CDs decorated silica column showed good separation performance for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and alkylbenzenes under RPLC mode. Hydrophilic compounds including sulfonamides, nucleosides and nucleobases also achieved good resolution in HILIC mode. Hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions play major retaining roles in RPLC, whereas hydrophilic partitioning and hydrogen bond interactions turn to the main retention interactions under HILIC mode. This C18-CDs/SiO2 column was applied for the fast detection of chloramphenicol in milk without complex sample pretreatment process. Quantitative relationship between the peak area and the concentration of chloramphenicol was established with linear equation of A = 1677c + 173. Satisfactory spiked recoveries in the range of 94.1–109.0% were obtained. This work not only proposes a simple method for improving the polarity of C18 column through forming octadecane into CDs, but also provides novel CDs with certain hydrophobicity/hydrophily suitable for mixed-mode chromatography.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Reversed phase liquid chromatography
Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography
Mixed-mode chromatography
Retention mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of C18-CDs and C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of synthesis process of C18-CDs and C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase.
TEM of C18-CDs (a), TEM and LSCM images of C18-CDs/SiO2 (b, c).
FT-IR (a) and thermogravimetric weight loss curves (b).

(A) Chromatograms for the separation of alkylbenzenes on (a) commercial C18 column, (b) C18/SiO2 column and (c) C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Analytes: (1) toluene, (2) ethylbenzene, (3) propyl benzene, (4) butylbenzene, (5) pentyl benzene; Mobile phase: (a) MeOH/H2O (90:10, v/v), (b) MeOH/H2O (85:10, v/v), (c) MeOH/H2O (80:10, v/v); Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1 (B) Plots of log k versus the number of methylene groups of alkylbenzenes under mobile phase containing 60, 70 and 80% MeOH.

Chromatograms for the separation of PAHs on (a) commercial C18 column, (b) C18/SiO2 column and (c) C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Analytes: (1) naphthalene, (2) biphenyl, (3) acenaphthene, (4) phenanthrene, (5) fluoranthene, (6) pyrene, (7) triphenylene; Mobile phase: (a) MeOH/H2O (90:10, v/v), (b) MeOH/H2O (85:15, v/v), (c) MeOH/H2O (85:15, v/v); Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.
Plots of log k versus volume fraction of ACN in mobile phase.

Chromatograms for the separation of nucleosides and nucleobases on (a) commercial NH2 column (b) C18/SiO2 column and (c) C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Analytes: (1) thymine, (2) uracil, (3) thymidine, (4) uridine, (5) adenosine, (6) cytosine, (7) adenine, (8) cytidine, (9) inosine, (10) 6-hydroxypurine, (11) guanine, (12) guanosine, (13) 2,6-dihydroxypurine, (14) xanthosine; Mobile phase is composed with A (ACN) and B (20 mM ammonium acetate): (a) 0–2 min 95% A, 2–10 min 90% A, 10–15 min 80% A, 15–20 min 70% A, 20–30 min 60% A; (b) 0–10 min 95% A, 10–30 min 70% A; (c) 0–5 min 95% A, 5–10 min 90% A, 10–15 min 80% A, 15–25 min 70% A; Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.

Chromatograms for the separation of sulfonamides on (a) commercial NH2 column, (b) C18/SiO2 column and (c) C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Analytes: (1) sulfanilamide, (2) sulfapyridine, (3) sulfamethazine, (4) sulfamerazine, (5) sulfadiazine, (6) sulfadimethoxine, (7) sulfadoxine, (8) sulfathiazole, (9) sulfisoxazole; Mobile phase is composed with A (ACN) and B (20 mM ammonium acetate): (a) 0–5 min 95% A, 5–15 min 90% A, 15–20 min 80% A; (b) 0–16 min 95% A; (c) 0–5 min 95% A, 5–10 min 90% A, 10–20 min 85% A; Flow rate: 1.0 mL min−1.
Experimental log k versus calculated log k plots for C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase under RPLC (a) and HILIC (b) modes.

(a) Chromatograms of milk extracts without and with added chloramphenicol, Mobile phase: acetonitrile/20 mM ammonium acetate (95:5, v/v); (b) Linear relationship between peak area and concentration of chloramphenicol.
Specific surface area, pore volume and pore size data.
Chromatographic parameters of tested PAHs on C18-CDs/SiO2 column.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: k: retention factor, k = (tR-t0)/t0, tR: retention time, t0: dead time.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Rs: resolution, Rs = 2(tR2-tR1)/(W1 + W2), W: peak width at peak base.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: T: tailing factor, T = W0.05 h/2d1, W0.05h: peak width at 5% height, d1: the distance from the summit to the leading edge of peak.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: N: number of theoretical plates, N = 16(tR/W)2.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: System coefficients and statistics of C18-CDs/SiO2 column.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of octadecylamine-derived carbon dots and application in reversed phase/hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey Laboratory of Chemistry of Northwestern Plant Resources and Key laboratory for Natural Medicine of Gansu Province, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to make up for the deficiencies of traditional C18 column for separating strong polar compounds, combined with the good hydrophilicity of carbon dots (CDs), novel octadecylamine-derived CDs denoted as C18-CDs are designed, synthesized and applied in RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography with good separation performance towards both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds. C18-CDs are synthesized by simple one-step solvothermal method using octadecylamine and citric acid as carbon sources, and C18-CDs with proper polarity are collected through column chromatography purification. This C18-CDs decorated silica column showed good separation performance for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and alkylbenzenes under RPLC mode. Hydrophilic compounds including sulfonamides, nucleosides and nucleobases also achieved good resolution in HILIC mode. Hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions play major retaining roles in RPLC, whereas hydrophilic partitioning and hydrogen bond interactions turn to the main retention interactions under HILIC mode. This C18-CDs/SiO2 column was applied for the fast detection of chloramphenicol in milk without complex sample pretreatment process. Quantitative relationship between the peak area and the concentration of chloramphenicol was established with linear equation of A = 1677c + 173. Satisfactory spiked recoveries in the range of 94.1–109.0% were obtained. This work not only proposes a simple method for improving the polarity of C18 column through forming octadecane into CDs, but also provides novel CDs with certain hydrophobicity/hydrophily suitable for mixed-mode chromatography.

ce:section.ce:para: High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is an important and universal analysis method because of high accuracy. Achieving satisfactory separation of many kinds of analytes using one chromatographic column is the goal in chromatographic separation. Mixed-mode chromatography with multiple retention mechanisms and excellent separation performance towards various kinds of analytes under different separation modes has been the research emphasis in separation science [1,2].

C18 column with good separation performance towards hydrophobic and weak polar compounds, has developed as the most widely used chromatographic column. However, traditional C18 column shows some deficiencies for separating hydrophilic analytes facing the demand of bioanalysis [3,4]. To make up for the deficiencies of conventional C18 column, various polar groups-modified C18 columns have been developed and applied in mixed-mode chromatography [5–8]. Nanomaterials including graphene oxide [9], graphene quantum dots [10], molybdenum disulfide quantum dots [11] and porous organic cage [12] have also been composited with C18 to construct modified C18 column. These polar C18 columns make great contributions to the improvement of the separation performance towards strong polar compounds, but their preparation involves multi-step modification processes of small molecules onto silica or synthesis of complex material, which is relatively complicated facing the development trend of green chemistry. Thus, nanomaterials with easy and low-cost synthesis method as well as applicability in mixed-mode chromatography, are urgently needed in separation science.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with excellent fluorescence properties, have been widely applied in biological imaging and sensing [13], whereas less attention has been paid to their separation performance. According to the properties of CDs, they are good candidates for constructing mixed-mode chromatographic materials. First, CDs have highly delocalized conjugate system and abundant functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and alkoxyl, which can provide π-π stacking, dipole-dipole and hydrogen bond retention interactions [14]. Multiple interactions are helpful to enhance the separation selectivity towards complex samples. Second, CDs have high chemical, mechanical and thermal stability, the small size makes CDs easier to be modified onto the silica gel supports [15]. Last but not least, the synthesis methods of CDs are simple and low cost [16]. By adjusting the type of carbon sources, different kinds of CDs can be obtained. In recent years, various types of CDs have been used as HPLC stationary phases and presented excellent separation performance [17–21]. However, the application of CDs in HPLC is limited to HILIC since their excellent hydrophilic properties.

Considering the deficiencies of C18 column for separating hydrophilic compounds, and the excellent hydrophilic properties of CDs, we think that CDs derived from carbon sources containing octadecane have great prospect in mixed-mode chromatography. CDs derived from alkanes such as dodecylamine and octadecylamine have been synthesized [22,23], however, these alkylated CDs are not ideal CDs for mixed-mode chromatography due to their strong hydrophobicity. So far, we have not found any examples about the application of C18-derived CDs in mixed-mode chromatography. Hence, novel C18-derived CDs are synthesized by simple one-step solvothermal method. Octadecylamine was selected as one of the precursors to achieve the embedding of alkyl chain into CDs. Citric acid as another carbon source was used to induce hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups into CDs. The synthesized CDs mixture were further purified by column chromatography and C18-CDs with proper polarity were collected and decorated onto silica gels as mixed-mode stationary phase. Experimental process is depicted in Fig. 1 . The separation performance was evaluated in both RPLC and HILIC modes. Retention mechanism was explored by linear solvation energy relationship (LSER). The applicability in real sample analysis and the stability of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column were also investigated systematically.

Silica gels (100 Å, 5 μm) were provided by Fuji Silysia Chemical Ltd (Japan). Octadecanamine was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)−3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) were provided by Meryer Chemical Technology Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All standard chemicals analyzed by HPLC were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China).

The Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were conducted on a Hitachi HT7700 TEM (HITACHI, Japan). Laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) images were acquired using a FLUOVIEW FV1200 microscope (OLYMPUS, Japan). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement was carried on a NOVA 2200e instrument (Quantachrome, USA). Contact angle test was performed on KRÜSS DSA25 instrument (KRÜSS, Germany). Results of thermogravimetric analysis were obtained on a METTLER TOLEDO TGA2 system (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland).

The designed C18-CDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method inspired by references [22–24]. 3.0 g citric acid and 1.2 g octadecanamine were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol, then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the CDs mixture with different polarity was further purified by column chromatography using methanol/chloroform (1:20) as eluent to collect C18-CDs with proper polarity.

The C18-CDs with proper polarity were modified onto silica gels by simple acylation reaction between carboxyl groups of CDs and amino groups of aminosilica (NH2/SiO2) with EDC/NHS as coupling agent. The preparation method of NH2/SiO2 is according to our previous reports [25]. To prepare C18-CDs/SiO2 composites, 500 mg C18-CDs were first dispersed in 200 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide, then 0.38 g EDC and 0.22 g NHS were added to activate the carboxyl groups of C18-CDs. After 30 min, NH2/SiO2 (2.5 g) were added to above dispersion and continuously stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. Finally, the C18-CDs/SiO2 composites were washed using ethanol and methanol, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h under vacuum. The whole preparation procedure of C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase is simple without using any organic reagent with high toxicity.

A C18/SiO2 stationary phase was also prepared by bonding octadecanoic acid onto aminosilica to be used as a compare for better present the separation performance of C18-CDs. The preparation method was as same as that of C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase. It must be noted that this C18/SiO2 stationary phase was prepared without end-capping, thus it still has certain polarity since the presence of residual silanol groups.

Isochoric carbon tetrachloride and 1,4-dioxane was used as homogenate solvent and methanol as propulsion solvent, the chromatographic materials were loaded into stainless steel tube (150 × 4.6 mm i.d.) under the pressure of 40 MPa.

Chromatographic performance evaluation was carried out on Thermo UltiMate 3000 HPLC system. The separation performance of C18-CDs/SiO2 column was compared with commercial Purospher STAR RP-18 endcapped Hibar RT (5 μm, 150 × 4.6 mm i.d.) column and Purospher STAR NH2 LiChroCART (5 μm, 150 × 4.6 mm i.d.) column. The column temperature was set at 25 °C with UV detection wavelength of 254 nm. The concentrations of injected standard chemicals are 20–100 μmol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of C18-CDs and C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of C18-CDs can be clearly observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, C18-CDs are spherical and mono-dispersed particles with diameters in the range of 2–6 nm, a mean size about 4.5 nm. A thin layer of CDs attached to the surface of silica gel can be clearly seen from the TEM image of C18-CDs/SiO2 composite (Fig. 2b). Bright fluorescence can also be seen on the C18-CDs/SiO2 composite through LSCM imaging (Fig. 2c). All of these indicates the successful preparation of C18-CDs modified silica.

The chemical composition of C18-CDs and C18-CDs/SiO2 composite were characterized by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the peak located at 3422 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH. The prominent peaks at 2919/2849 cm−1 are contributed to the symmetric and asymmetric CH stretching vibration, and the peak at 1465 cm−1 may be derived from -CH2 deformation vibration. It should be noted that the peak at 717 cm−1 indicates the number of methylene is at least 4, which indicates the existence of long alkyl chain in CDs. The peak at 1707 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C=O in COOH. The peak at 1650 cm−1 which is partly overlapped with the peak of COOH is the characteristic peak of amide bond. The peak at 1104 cm−1 can be derived from the stretching vibration of CO-C. The presence of characteristic peaks of CH2, NH-C=O, COOH in C18-CDs/SiO2 composite indicates the decoration of C18-CDs onto NH2/SiO2. According to the FT-IR characterization results, C18-CDs and C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase with functional groups including alkyl chain, hydroxy, carboxyl and alkoxyl groups are prepared successfully.

Analyze the thermogravimetric curves depicted in Fig. 3b, very low mass lost was detected below 300 °C for NH2/SiO2, C18/SiO2 and C18-CDs/SiO2 composites, might be due to the volatilization of reagent. Above 300 °C, the organic components of C18-CDs/SiO2 and C18/SiO2 started to decompose, the initial degradation rate of C18/SiO2 was higher than C18-CDs/SiO2, which meant C18-CDs/SiO2 had higher thermal stability. NH2/SiO2 did not degrade violently until the temperature reached 450 °C, which demonstrated the high thermostability of NH2/SiO2 substrate. Comparing the mass loss of NH2/SiO2 and C18-CDs/SiO2, the loaded amount of C18-CDs on silica was approximately 12%, which was higher than the loaded C18 on silica (about 8%). This indicated the satisfactory bonding efficiency of C18-CDs on silica.

BET data of this C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase were listed in Table 1 , increased specific surface area and decreased pore volume and pore size could be observed after modification of C18-CDs onto NH2/SiO2, implied that besides surface modification, C18-CDs were also entered into the porous channels of the silica gels.

Traditional approach suggested by Engelhardt et al. for characterization of silanol activity was used to evaluate the C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Aniline, phenol, toluene and ethylbenzene were standard analytes for evaluating silanol activity and hydrophobic selectivity [26]. At specified mobile phase, the relative retention of toluene and ethylbenzene was a measure of hydrophobic selectivity, and the relative retention of aniline and phenol was the characterization of silanol activity. Generally, the elution of aniline before phenol indicates low silanol activity [26–28]. The chromatogram of the test analytes on the developed C18-CDs/SiO2 column was shown in Fig. S1, the α phenol/aniline was calculated to be 1.48, and the α ethylbenzene/toluene was 1.51, which indicated that most silanol groups have been sheltered.

The retention behavior and separation performance of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column under RPLC mode was evaluated by polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and alkylbenzenes with commercial C18 column and homemade C18/SiO2 column as a compare. As shown in Fig. 4 A, five alkylbenzenes with one methylene difference in the molecular structure obtained good separation on both commercial C18 column, homemade C18/SiO2 column and this C18-CDs/SiO2 column. The elution order of alkylbenzenes was consistent with their hydrophobicity, which indicated that hydrophobic interaction played an important role on the chromatographic separation. The hydrophobic selectivity of C18-CDs/SiO2 column originates from the alkyl chain embedded in CDs. As shown in Fig. 4B, the logarithm of retention factor (k) increased linearly with the number of methylene groups under different mobile phases. This is a typical characteristic of RPLC and can be always observed on traditional C18 column [28,29]. This C18-CDs/SiO2 column has comparable separation performance and similar retention behavior with C18 column.

Though the developed C18-CDs/SiO2 column had similar retention behavior with C18 column, its hydrophobicity was found to be weaker than homemade C18/SiO2 column and commercial C18 column. In the same retention time, the order of the eluent strength used for these three columns was commercial C18 column > homemade C18/SiO2 column > C18-CDs/SiO2 column. The homemade C18/SiO2 column was prepared without end-capping, thus its hydrophobicity was weaker than commercial C18 column. The C18-CDs/SiO2 column showed the weakest hydrophobic retention interaction since there were many polar oxygen-containing groups in C18-CDs.

Seven PAHs were well separated on C18-CDs/SiO2 column within 12 min, while they were not completely separated on commercial C18 column and homemade C18/SiO2 column within the same analysis time, as shown in Fig. 5 . Chromatographic separation parameters including k, resolution, column efficiency, selectivity factor and tailing factor are listed in Table 2 . The better separation result obtained from C18-CDs/SiO2 column was probably because the π-π stacking interaction between C18-CDs and PAHs since CDs possess the highly delocalized conjugated system. The order of eluent strength used for separating PAHs on the three columns was commercial C18 column > homemade C18/SiO2 column = C18-CDs/SiO2 column. Different from the case in separating alkylbenzenes, C18-CDs/SiO2 column with weaker hydrophobicity had comparable retention ability with homemade C18/SiO2 column towards PAHs. This phenomenon again verified the contribution of π-π stacking interaction on the retention of C18-CDs/SiO2 column.

It is expected that the developed C18-CDs/SiO2 column could perform both RPLC and HILIC behaviors. Three classic alkaloids were chosen as model solutes to investigate its retention behavior under mobile phases with different acetonitrile content. It is well known that the retention behavior of HILIC is somewhat opposite to RPLC [26,30]. In binary mobile phase system, water is the stronger eluent in HILIC, whereas is the weaker eluent in RPLC mode. As shown in Fig. 6 , with the increase of the proportion of acetonitrile in mobile phase, the retention time had two change laws. First, retention decreases with the ACN proportion increasing from 20 to 60%, displaying the retention behavior of RPLC; and then increases with the ACN proportion constantly increasing from 60 to 95%, showing the retention characteristic of HILIC. This retention behavior reflects the mode conversion of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column from RPLC to HILIC. We speculate that the surface of C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase should be occupied by a water-rich layer when ACN content in mobile phase exceeds 60%, thus hydrophilic interaction emerges. It can be concluded that this C18-CDs/SiO2 column possesses typical features of RPLC/HILIC mixed-mode chromatography with the help of multiple functional groups in the prepared C18-CDs.

The separation performance of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column in HILIC mode was evaluated by some hydrophilic solutes including sulfonamides, nucleosides and nucleobases. Commercial NH2 column and homemade C18/SiO2 column were chosen as a compare to better reflect the separation performance and hydrophilic retention interaction of C18-CDs. Fig. 7 presented the chromatograms of nucleosides and nucleobases acquired on the three columns. 14 nucleosides and nucleobases achieved good separation on this C18-CDs/SiO2 column within 25 min. However, they were not completely separated on commercial NH2 column and homemade C18/SiO2 column. The better separation performance of C18-CDs/SiO2 column was probably because the synergistic effect of multiple functional groups including carboxyl, hydroxyl and aminos. According to the eluent strength used for the three columns, the order of hydrophily intensity was commercial NH2 column > C18-CDs/SiO2 column > homemade C18/SiO2 column. The homemade C18/SiO2 column without end-capping had residual silanol groups and the formed amide groups, thus it presented hydrophilic property. But the hydrophilic interaction on homemade C18/SiO2 column was too weak to achieve adequate retention for effective separation even using very weak eluent (95% acetonitrile/5% ammonium acetate aqueous solution). While C18-CDs/SiO2 column prepared by the same method as C18/SiO2 column showed satisfactory retention and separation performance, which reflected the retention role of C18-CDs. Moreover, the different retention order of analytes on C18-CDs/SiO2 column also revealed the different retention interactions originated from C18-CDs. Due to the presence of various functional groups and conjugate system in C18-CDs, the retention mechanism of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column should be multiple including hydrophilic partition, hydrogen bond and π-π stacking interactions.

Take the separation of sulfonamides as an example (Fig. 8 ), the retention order of sulfamerazine and sulfamethazine was reversed with their hydrophobicity, which indicated that it was the hydrophilic interaction not the hydrophobic interaction played the role on retention. The retention order of sulfadiazine and sulfadoxine on NH2 column and C18/SiO2 column was coincident with their hydrophily, but was reversed on C18-CDs/SiO2 column, which again reflected the different retention mechanism of C18-CDs. The hydrophily of sulfadiazine was stronger than sulfadoxine, thus it had stronger retention on NH2 column and C18/SiO2 column. But sulfadoxine had longer retention time on C18-CDs/SiO2 column, this might be due to that the side chain group of methoxy which acted as electron donor group enhanced the electron density of sulfadoxine and thus the π-π stacking interaction with C18-CDs. Hydrogen bond interaction between methoxies of sulfadoxine and oxygen-containing groups in C18-CDs might be another reason for the stronger retention of sulfadoxine on C18-CDs/SiO2 column. This phenomenon again reflected the multiple retention interactions of C18-CDs.

In conclusion, this C18-CDs/SiO2 column shows excellent separation performance in both RPLC mode and HILIC mode towards both hydrophobic and hydrophilic compounds. This may be due to the synergistic effect of various kinds of functional groups in C18-CDs such as alkyl chains, carboxy and hydroxy groups. Hydrophobic interaction plays the most important role on retention in RPLC, while hydrophilic interaction contributes most on the retention in HILIC. Π-Π stacking interaction plays a role in both RPLC and HILIC modes.

LSER has been widely used to study the distribution characteristics of solutes between mobile phase and stationary phase from the perspective of individual retention interaction [31,32]. An equation proposed by Chirita et al. [33] on the basis of original Abraham equation [34] can express the application principle of LSER in understanding the retention mechanism of chromatographic column. log k = eE + sS + aA + bB + vV + d − D + d + D ++ c

In the equation, capital letters were the solute descriptors, lower case letters were the system constants. E is the excess molar refraction and models polarizability contributions from n and π electrons. S is the solute dipolarity/polarizability. A and B are the overall hydrogen-bond acidity and basicity. V is the McGowan characteristic volume. D − and D +represents the negative or positive charge carried by ionic and zwitterionic solutes. c is the model intercept term, is not assigned any chemical significance.

To understand the difference in retention mechanism under different chromatographic modes, LSER model was established under RPLC and HILIC modes using 25 model molecules consisted of hydrophilic, hydrophobic and polar solutes. Solute descriptor values gained from the published literatures [32,33,35] were listed in Table S1. As for the mobile phase condition, ammonium acetate buffer (20 mM, pH7.0) was used as aqueous phase. The percentage of acetonitrile in RPLC and HILIC mode was 40% and 90%, respectively. The absolute values of system constants reflect the contribution of a specified interaction to the distribution of solutes between stationary phase and mobile phase. A positive system constant means stronger interaction between solute and stationary phase. A negative system constant indicates solutes are more likely to distribute in the mobile phase. System coefficients obtained from RPLC and HILIC modes are listed in Table 3 . The adjusted coefficient of determination (R2 adj) is 0.94 and 0.92 for the established LSER formula under RPLC and HILIC, respectively. It can be observed that there are obvious differences in system constants between RPLC and HILIC modes, which indicates that the C18-CDs/SiO2 column presents different retention mechanism under different separation modes. As presented in Fig. 9 , experimental log k and calculated log k have good consistency, which signifies the reliability of the established LSER model for explaining the retention mechanism.

The e parameter reflects the distributional role of solutes between stationary phase and mobile phase based on π-π stacking interaction. Positive and large e values are obtained in both RPLC and HILIC modes, signifying the great contributions of π-π stacking interaction on retention. This is in accord with the experimental results that the C18-CDs/SiO2 column presents good aromatic selectivity attributed to the conjugate system in C18-CDs. It seems that π-π stacking interaction contributes more for the retention under RPLC mode comparing the absolute values of all the system constants.

The s coefficient represents the retention capacity of stationary phase or mobile phase towards dipolar and/or polarizable solutes. In the present case, a negative s value is obtained under RPLC mode, revealing stronger dipole-dipole interaction between solutes and mobile phase. This is reasonable since the used mobile phase containing high proportion of water is much more polar than the stationary phase. However, a positive s value is received under HILIC mode, which means the stationary phase has stronger dipole-dipole interaction with solutes than mobile phase. This is because an aqueous layer comes into being on the surface of C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase when mobile phase containing high proportion of organic solvents is used under HILIC mode. The water-rich layer acts as pseudo-stationary phase and thus greatly enhances the polar interaction with polarizable solutes. The variation of the system constant values explains the retention behavior of the stationary phase in different separation modes theoretically.

The a and b system constants represent the hydrogen bonding interactions. The a coefficient presents the hydrogen-bond basicity or hydrogen-bond acceptor property of the stationary phase, which is complementary to acidic solutes with large A values. Whereas b represents the hydrogen-bond acidity or hydrogen-bond donor property of the stationary phase. Both a and b coefficients are negative in RPLC, while they turn to positive values under HILIC mode, which shows the contributions of hydrogen-bond interaction on the retention in HILIC. The positive contributions of hydrogen-bond interactions may originate from the immobilized aqueous layer and the oxygen-containing functional groups in C18-CDs. Comparing the absolute values of a and b, it is observed that b values are much larger than a values no matter in RPLC or HILIC mode. This is in accord with previous reports that the hydrogen-bond donor property played an important role on the retention of a silica stationary phase, while the hydrogen-bond accept character was not significant [36]. Large and negative b value is the typical feature of conventional C18 stationary phase [4,37]. The large and positive b coefficient in HILIC mainly originates from the aqueous pseudo-stationary phase since the strong Lewis acidic character of water.

The v system constant on behalf of the hydrophobic interaction. In RPLC mode, a relatively large and positive v value is achieved, which signifies the main retention interaction of hydrophobic interaction. The hydrophobic retention capacity of this C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase stems from the aliphatic chain in C18-CDs. It is found that b and v coefficients are always large and opposite in partitioning systems composed of water [33]. Generally, b is negative and v is positive in RPLC systems [37]. While in HILIC mode, b is positive and v is negative [35,38], which confirms the reversed retention behavior of HILIC and RPLC.

The d system constant reflects the contributions of electrostatic interaction in chromatographic system. d −represents the electrostatic attraction interaction towards anionic compounds, while d +signifies the electrostatic attraction interaction towards cationic compounds. In RPLC, d − and d + values are very small and their contribution to retention can be ignored. However, the values of d − and d +increase a lot in HILIC. This means charged compounds have strong retention on this C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase under HILIC mode, which originates from the enhanced solubility of ionic compounds in the water-rich layer and reduced solubility in the acetonitrile-rich mobile phase. This phenomenon can also be explained by hydrophilic partitioning.

In conclusion, under RPLC mode, the main retention interactions are π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions. In HILIC mode, except for π-π stacking interaction, most polar-type interactions including hydrogen-bond, dipole-dipole and hydrophilic partitioning interactions make contributions to the retention on this C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase. The theoretical data is in keeping with the experimental phenomenon that this C18-CDs/SiO2 stationary phase possesses good hydrophobic selectivity and aromatic selectivity under RPLC mode, and good hydrophilic property in HILIC. It is interesting that the chromatographic column can switch its retention interactions under different separation modes automatically, thus to achieve efficient separation towards various kinds of compounds.

The applicability of the developed C18-CDs/SiO2 column for real sample analysis was tested by detecting residual chloramphenicol in milk. Chloramphenicol is an effective therapeutic agent for the treatment of mastitis in cattle and is banned for use in food-producing animals in many countries [39]. Generally, the detection of chloramphenicol in milk by HPLC based on C18 column needs a series of sample pretreatment process including extraction by ethyl acetate, degrease by n-hexane and purification by solid phase extraction column [39,40]. In this case, the analysis of chloramphenicol can be achieved fast without complex pretreatment process. The milk (called Modern Meadow) purchased from local supermarket (RT Mart, Qingdao, CHINA) was extracted by ethyl acetate, then was injected for analysis directly after filtered with nylon filter membrane (0.22 μm). As shown in Fig. 10 , most interfering substances were coeluted within 3 min under the HILIC condition, while chloramphenicol with certain polarity had appropriate retention and could be separated well with other interferents. No chloramphenicol was detected in the milk. The quantitative relationship was established since the peak area increased linearly with the concentration of chloramphenicol, the linear equation was fitted to be A = 1677c + 173 during the concentration range of 1–20 μg/L, with the correlation coefficient of 0.998. The spiked recoveries (Table S3) were in the range of 94.1−109.0%, with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 6.1%, which demonstrated the developed method based on this C18-CDs/SiO2 column was reliable.

A series of stability test under extreme pH conditions, different temperatures and long-time elution were carried out to evaluate the durability of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column. First, the column stability after long-time washing by eluent was tested. As shown in Fig. S2, the retention time and column efficiency of all model analytes had no obvious change after continuous washing with 85% MeOH/H2O as eluent over 96 h. In fact, this C18-CDs/SiO2 column has been used for nearly six months with more than 1000 injections during the evaluation process, no significant reduction of column performance was observed.

Second, the tolerance with acid and alkaline of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column was tested. Considering the use restrictions of silica gels were in the pH range of 2.0–8.0, the pH of eluent was adjusted to 2.8 and 8.0, respectively. The separation test was conducted at regular intervals (Figs. S3 and S4), no obvious loss of chromatographic performance was found after continuously washing for 72 h. The RSD of k was below 1.13 and 1.09% under acid and basic conditions, which indicated the good tolerance with acid and alkaline of the C18-CDs/SiO2 column.

Third, the thermal stability of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column was tested. To avoid violent volatilization of the mobile phase, temperature was set at 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, respectively. No obvious decrease of separation performance was found after the temperature treatment for 24 h (Fig. S5). It needs to be noted that retention usually decreases as the temperature increases.

Finally, the repeatability and reproducibility were tested using PAHs as analytes. The RSDs of k, column efficiency and peak area are listed in Table S3. The RSDs of k were below 0.32% (n = 10, run-to-run), 0.41% (n = 3, day-to-day) and 6.11% (n = 3, column-to-column). After a series of tests, this C18-CDs/SiO2 column meets the needs of daily analysis.

Novel polar C18-CDs were designed and synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using octadecylamine and citric acid as carbon sources. The C18-CDs were further purified by column chromatography and modified onto silica forming a novel C18-CDs/SiO2 mixed-mode chromatography stationary phase. This C18-CDs/SiO2 column showed good hydrophobic and aromatic selectivity for alkylbenzenes and PAHs under RPLC mode, and satisfactory separation performance for hydrophilic compounds including sulfonamides, nucleosides and nucleobases under HILIC mode. Moreover, the developed C18-CDs/SiO2 chromatographic column had comparable separation performance with commercial RPLC and HILIC columns under certain conditions. Through establishing LSER model, it was concluded that π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions were the main retention interactions in RPLC mode, when the separation mode switched to HILIC, most polar interactions including hydrophilic partitioning, hydrogen bond and dipole-dipole interactions turned to the main retention interactions. The conclusion draw from LSER were in accord with the experimental phenomenon. The applicability of this C18-CDs/SiO2 column for real sample analysis was tested by detecting chloramphenicol in milk. This C18-CDs/SiO2 column can achieve the fast detection of chloramphenicol in milk without complex sample pretreatment process. A good quantitative relationship between the peak area and the concentration of chloramphenicol was established with linear equation of A = 1677c + 173, correlation coefficient of 0.998. Satisfactory spiked recoveries in the range of 94.1−109.0% were obtained, which demonstrated the developed method based on this C18-CDs/SiO2 column was reliable. This work demonstrated that CDs derived from alkanes with proper hydrophobicity/hydrophily have great prospect in mixed-mode chromatography.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2020QB087), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901766), the Talents of High Level Scientific Research Foundation, Qingdao Agricultural University (No. 6651120016), and the Support Plan on Science and Technology for Youth Innovation of Universities in Shandong Province (No. 2019KJM002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2021.462548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of chloramphenicol in honey and milk by HPLC coupled with aptamer-functionalized Fe3O4/graphene oxide magnetic solid-phase extraction
